event: According to the Malay Annals, the prince Sang Nila Utama founds the settlement of Singapura ('Lion City') on the island of Temasek.
Temasek, a thriving trading port at the tip of the Malay Peninsula, sends a trade mission to the Mongol Yuan court of China.
Chinese trader Wang Dayuan describes Temasek as a populated trading settlement with Chinese and indigenous inhabitants.
Parameswara, a Palembang prince, briefly rules Singapura before fleeing and later founding the Malacca Sultanate.
Singapura is sacked, likely by Majapahit or Siamese forces, and declines into a minor settlement under later Malay sultanates.
The Portuguese conquest of Malacca shifts regional power and further marginalizes the Singapore settlement.
Portuguese forces raid and burn down the trading settlement at the mouth of the Singapore River.
Sir Stamford Raffles of the British East India Company establishes a trading post on Singapore, signing a treaty with local Malay rulers.
The Anglo-Dutch Treaty and a treaty with the Sultan and Temenggong cede full sovereignty of Singapore to the British.
Singapore joins Penang and Malacca to form the Straits Settlements under British administration.
Singapore becomes the capital of the Straits Settlements, growing rapidly as a free port and immigration hub.
The Straits Settlements become a Crown Colony directly governed by the British Colonial Office in London.
The opening of the Suez Canal dramatically boosts Singapore's importance as a port on the Europe-Asia shipping route.
Raffles Hotel opens as the rubber and tin trade fuels Singapore's economic boom and its rise as a major entrepot.
The Johor-Singapore Causeway opens, linking the island to the Malay Peninsula by road and rail.
Japanese forces capture Singapore in February, beginning a brutal occupation and renaming the island Syonan-to.
British forces return and reoccupy Singapore in September following Japan's surrender at the end of World War II.
Singapore becomes a separate Crown Colony as the Straits Settlements are dissolved.
Singapore holds its first elections to a Legislative Council, though with a limited franchise.
Lee Kuan Yew and colleagues found the People's Action Party (PAP), which would come to dominate Singaporean politics.
A new constitution expands self-government and the Hock Lee bus riots highlight rising labour and anti-colonial tensions.
Singapore attains full internal self-government and Lee Kuan Yew becomes the first Prime Minister.
Singapore joins the Federation of Malaysia together with Malaya, Sabah, and Sarawak.
Racial riots break out in Singapore, deepening tensions between the PAP and the central Malaysian government.
Singapore separates from Malaysia and becomes a fully independent and sovereign republic on 9 August.
Singapore is admitted to the United Nations in September, shortly after gaining independence.
Singapore becomes a founding member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN).
The Jurong Industrial Estate and aggressive industrialization policies drive rapid export-led economic growth.
The last British military forces withdraw from Singapore, ending its role as a major imperial naval base.
Singapore Changi Airport opens, soon becoming one of the world's leading aviation hubs.
Opposition candidate J. B. Jeyaretnam wins a by-election, breaking the PAP's complete hold on Parliament.
The Mass Rapid Transit (MRT) system begins operations, transforming urban transport in the city-state.
Goh Chok Tong succeeds Lee Kuan Yew as Prime Minister, ending Lee's 31-year tenure.
Singapore holds its first direct presidential election, with Ong Teng Cheong becoming the first elected president.
Singapore weathers the Asian Financial Crisis with relative stability owing to strong reserves and prudent policy.
Singapore is struck by the SARS outbreak, prompting strict public health measures across the city-state.
Lee Hsien Loong, son of Lee Kuan Yew, becomes the third Prime Minister of Singapore.
Singapore hosts the inaugural Formula One night race, the Singapore Grand Prix, boosting global tourism.
Two integrated resorts with casinos, Marina Bay Sands and Resorts World Sentosa, open and reshape the tourism economy.
Founding Prime Minister Lee Kuan Yew dies in March as the nation marks its 50th year of independence.
Singapore hosts the historic summit between US President Donald Trump and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un.
Singapore confronts the COVID-19 pandemic with strict 'circuit breaker' lockdown measures and aggressive contact tracing.
Lawrence Wong succeeds Lee Hsien Loong as Prime Minister, marking the first leadership handover beyond the founding generation's direct line.
